Training-time vs test-time compute comparison diagram

content.text: ) refers to the computational resources a model expends
—during inference—rather than during training. In Claude's context, this is the tokens and processing power used for
Test-time compute is the defining characteristic of
Large test-time compute budget (10–50× more tokens)
Higher latency, higher cost, better accuracy
"Spend more tokens reasoning to spend fewer attempts overall"
Math, code, logic benchmarks improve with reasoning depth
Multi-step planning, debugging benefit from extended thinking
Model can verify answers against criteria before finishing
Single reasoning call may replace multiple standard attempts
Better at navigating uncertain information than fast models
10–100× higher first-token latency (reasoning happens before visible output)
Uses 10–50× more tokens per call; full thinking tokens billed as output
Lower request rate due to longer processing time
Extended reasoning can deteriorate performance on some tasks (inverse scaling)

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         TRAINING-TIME COMPUTE (Historical Approach)         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Massive compute during training → Better model weights     │
│  Inference: Fast, single-pass generation                    │
│  Limited reasoning capability                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         TEST-TIME COMPUTE (Reasoning Model Approach)        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Standard model training + RL on verifiable problems        │
│  Inference: Allocate extra compute to internal reasoning    │
│  Model thinks through problem → Better answer               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Input Request
    ↓
[Reasoning Phase: Thinking Tokens] ← Test-time compute (billed as output)
    ↓
[Response Phase: Text Output Tokens] ← Standard output
    ↓
Final Answer
: Full reasoning chain billed as output tokens (not summary)
Two Test-Time Compute Modes in Claude
Model auto-decides depth based on complexity +
No reasoning benefit; waste of tokens
Subjective quality; reasoning training grounded in verifiable rewards

# ❌ Wrong: Budget too low
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=200,  # Too low—reasoning + answer won't fit
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000}
)

# ✅ Correct: Size budget for task complexity
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=8000,  # Generous budget for reasoning + answer
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 6000}  # 6K for thinking
)

# Adaptive test-time compute—model decides depth
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=16000,
    thinking={"type": "adaptive"},  # Always-on adaptive thinking
    output_config={"effort": "high"},  # Control reasoning depth
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Complex query"}]
)
Routing every request to reasoning model
Expect slower starts; cache when possible
Normalize for inference compute in benchmarks
— Use classifier/heuristic to detect if reasoning is needed
— Ensure thinking + answer fit (8K minimum for reasoning)
— Track actual reasoning cost vs. allocated budget
— Identify inverse scaling failure modes for your tasks
— Computational resources spent during generation, not training
Reasoning models are defined by test-time compute
— Claude uses extended thinking to allocate extra tokens for reasoning
— But single reasoning call may replace multiple standard attempts
— Math, code, logic benefit most; creative writing doesn't
— Higher first-token latency, but better accuracy on complex tasks
— Extended reasoning can deteriorate performance on some tasks
— Ensure thinking + answer both fit (8K–16K recommended)
The mental model: Think of test-time compute as
giving Claude "time to think" like a human expert

—instead of rushing to answer, it spends computational resources reasoning through the problem internally, similar to how you'd spend mental effort working through a complex math problem before writing the solution.

To understand why test-time compute works, you need to understand what it actually does to the model's output distribution. A standard language model learns a probability distribution over next tokens conditioned on previous context. When you ask it a question, it samples from that distribution and produces an answer. The problem is that the learned distribution conflates multiple possible reasoning paths into one blurry average, so the model often picks a plausible-sounding but incorrect path.

Test-time compute addresses this by giving the model space to explore before committing. During extended thinking, Claude generates a sequence of reasoning tokens that act as working memory—similar to writing notes on scratch paper before writing a final answer. Because these thinking tokens are sampled from the same model but conditioned on a reasoning context, the model can backtrack, reconsider, and self-correct in ways that single-pass generation cannot.

The reinforcement learning training that underlies extended thinking teaches the model to use this scratch space effectively. Rather than learning from supervised examples of correct reasoning paths, the model is rewarded when its reasoning leads to verifiably correct outputs. Over many training iterations, the model learns which kinds of internal reasoning chains actually produce correct answers, developing heuristics for when to double-check its work, when to try alternative approaches, and when it has reached a confident conclusion.
Scaling Laws: How Much Reasoning Is Enough?

One of the most practically important findings in test-time compute research is that performance often follows a log-linear scaling curve with the number of thinking tokens. Doubling the thinking budget tends to produce consistent but diminishing improvement on benchmark tasks—though the benefit varies dramatically by task type and model capability.

For verifiable tasks like mathematics or code synthesis, performance keeps improving as you add more thinking tokens up to a task-specific ceiling. A hard competition math problem may keep improving from 1K to 32K thinking tokens, while a straightforward algebra problem shows most of its improvement by 2K tokens and plateaus thereafter. This means you should tune your thinking budget to your actual task difficulty rather than using a universal value.

For ambiguous or subjective tasks, the scaling behavior is less reliable. The model may overthink and introduce false precision, or it may reason its way into an overly cautious answer that fails to serve the user. Research on inverse scaling in test-time compute shows that some task categories actually perform worse at very high thinking budgets—the model becomes over-deliberate and second-guesses correct intuitions.

# Empirical budget calibration pattern
# Run the same prompt at increasing budgets and compare results

def calibrate_thinking_budget(prompt, correct_answer, model="claude-opus-4-7"):
    """Find the minimum budget that reliably produces correct answers."""
    budgets = [1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16000]
    results = {}
    
    for budget in budgets:
        correct_count = 0
        for trial in range(5):  # 5 trials per budget level
            response = client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=budget + 2000,
                thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": budget},
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
            )
            answer = extract_answer(response)
            if answer == correct_answer:
                correct_count += 1
        results[budget] = correct_count / 5  # accuracy at this budget
    
    # Find minimum budget achieving >= 90% accuracy
    for budget, accuracy in results.items():
        if accuracy >= 0.90:
            return budget, results
    
    return budgets[-1], results  # use max if threshold not reached

Thinking tokens are billed at the same rate as output tokens but can consume a large portion of your budget. Understanding the full token economy is essential for building cost-effective applications.

When Claude uses extended thinking, the API response includes the full thinking content in the response object. You can access the token breakdown through the usage field, which reports both thinking tokens and non-thinking output tokens separately. The key insight is that the thinking tokens are generated before the visible response but both are billed as output tokens. For a call with a 10,000-token thinking budget, you might pay for 8,500 thinking tokens plus 1,200 response tokens, totaling 9,700 output tokens—roughly 5× more expensive than a non-thinking call of the same visible length.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=12000,
    thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8000},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Prove that there are infinitely many prime numbers."}]
)

# Inspect token usage breakdown
print(f"Input tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {response.usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"Thinking tokens: {response.usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens}")
print(f"Non-thinking output: {response.usage.output_tokens - response.usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens}")

# Cost calculation (example pricing)
INPUT_PRICE_PER_1M = 15.0   # example price per 1M input tokens
OUTPUT_PRICE_PER_1M = 75.0  # example price per 1M output tokens

cost = (
    response.usage.input_tokens * INPUT_PRICE_PER_1M / 1_000_000 +
    response.usage.output_tokens * OUTPUT_PRICE_PER_1M / 1_000_000
)
print(f"Estimated cost: ${cost:.4f}")

One of the most important UX challenges with test-time compute is the latency: the model generates thousands of thinking tokens before producing any visible output. Without streaming, users see nothing for potentially 30–60 seconds before the entire response arrives. Streaming addresses this by sending content incrementally, but it requires special handling for thinking blocks.

When you enable streaming with extended thinking, you receive content block deltas for both thinking blocks and text blocks. The streaming events follow a specific sequence: a content_block_start event marks the beginning of a thinking block, followed by content_block_delta events carrying incremental thinking text, then content_block_stop when thinking completes, and finally the same sequence for the text response. You can use this to show users a progress indicator during thinking and then stream the final answer.

A critical and often misunderstood aspect of extended thinking is how it interacts with multi-turn conversations. The thinking content from one turn must be handled carefully when building subsequent turns, because the API has strict requirements about how thinking blocks appear in conversation history.

The fundamental rule is that thinking blocks must be preserved exactly as returned by the API when they appear in message history. You cannot modify, summarize, or selectively include thinking blocks. If you pass an assistant message that contained thinking in the conversation history, the entire content array from that response—including all thinking blocks—must be passed back verbatim. Stripping or modifying thinking blocks causes API errors or silent degradation of reasoning quality.

However, you are not required to enable thinking on every turn of a multi-turn conversation. A common pattern is to use extended thinking for the first, most complex turn and then continue the conversation with standard generation for follow-up clarifications. The thinking content from the first turn becomes part of the conversation history and helps Claude understand the original reasoning when answering follow-up questions.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def multi_turn_with_thinking():
    """Multi-turn conversation with thinking on first turn."""
    messages = []
    
    # Turn 1: Complex analysis with extended thinking
    user_turn_1 = "Design a database schema for a multi-tenant SaaS application with usage billing."
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_turn_1})
    
    response_1 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-7",
        max_tokens=16000,
        thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000},
        messages=messages
    )
    
    # Preserve ALL content blocks including thinking
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response_1.content})
    
    # Turn 2: Follow-up without thinking (simpler question)
    user_turn_2 = "How would you handle database migrations when adding new billing tiers?"
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_turn_2})
    
    # No thinking needed for this clarification
    response_2 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-7",
        max_tokens=4000,
        # No thinking parameter - uses standard generation
        messages=messages
    )
    
    return {
        "initial_design": response_1.content,
        "migration_answer": response_2.content,
        "thinking_tokens_used": response_1.usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens
    }
Monitoring and Observability for Reasoning Calls

Production systems using extended thinking need robust monitoring that goes beyond simple token counting. You need to track reasoning efficiency (how much of the thinking budget was used vs. allocated), response quality over time, and the relationship between thinking depth and outcome quality for your specific use case.

A key observability metric is the thinking utilization ratio: the fraction of the allocated budget_tokens that were actually consumed. If the model consistently uses only 30% of its budget, you are over-provisioning and wasting money. If it consistently hits the cap, you may be under-provisioning and truncating useful reasoning. The optimal utilization for a given task type tends to be 60–85%—enough headroom that the model rarely hits the cap, but not so much that you are paying for unused capacity.
